text: While access to the right upper lobe structures is most commonly performed from an anterior to posterior approach, there are occasions when one needs to begin the dissection posteriorly in order to best deal with a centrally located tumor or one that abuts the minor fissure.
The posterior mediastinal pleura is open and the right mainstem and bronchus intermedius are exposed.
One can appreciate that the station seven lymph nodes were removed first.
Dissection is then continued by mobilizing the parenchyma off the upper lobe bronchus and dissecting the nodal packet between the upper lobe bronchus and bronchus intermedius, shown here.
During this nodal dissection, one should always dissect between the bronchus and the node to best avoid injury to the posterior ascending artery.
The bronchus is then dissected away from the pulmonary artery branches which are anterior such that an instrument can be passed around the right upper lobe bronchus.
The pulmonary artery is mobilized with a combination of blunt and sharp dissection.
This dissection can be somewhat lengthy.
The real time to complete the dissection depicted in this video was 22 minutes, condensed here down to four minutes.

Other tools such as the endokitner are very useful in dissecting the fascial tissues away from the tips of the instrument.
